differences.title: 1. Data Fetching
differences.leftDesc: Clients request exactly the data they need, reducing over-fetching.
differences.rightDesc: Endpoints return fixed data structures; clients might get too much or too little.

differences.title: 2. Endpoint Structure
differences.leftDesc: Typically one endpoint handles all queries and mutations for a resource.
differences.rightDesc: Multiple URLs are used for different resources and actions.

differences.title: 3. Versioning
differences.leftDesc: Schema evolution allows adding fields without breaking existing clients.
differences.rightDesc: API changes often require new versions (v1, v2) to avoid breaking changes.

differences.title: 4. Type System
differences.leftDesc: Defines a strict schema, ensuring data integrity and predictable responses.
differences.rightDesc: Data formats (like JSON) are flexible; validation is often application-level.

differences.title: 5. Tooling & Ecosystem
differences.leftDesc: Rich tooling for introspection, auto-completion, and code generation is common.
differences.rightDesc: Extensive libraries and established patterns for various languages and platforms.

differences.title: 6. Caching
differences.leftDesc: Client-side caching requires more sophisticated logic due to varied queries.
differences.rightDesc: Leverages standard HTTP caching mechanisms effectively due to fixed URLs.

differences.title: 7. Performance
differences.leftDesc: Minimizes network requests and payload size by design.
differences.rightDesc: Can lead to larger payloads and more requests if not optimized carefully.
